transcript.presenting_concern: Communication breakdown — Gottman method four horsemen

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Kevin, Maria, good evening. Good to see you both. How has the week been since our last session?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Better than last week, I think. Noticeably better, actually. We did the exercise you suggested — the daily appreciation check-in.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Some days were more natural than others. But we did it every day, which is a first. Seven for seven.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Seven for seven. That consistency matters more than perfection. Even on the days it felt forced, you were building a habit. Tell me — what did it look like? How did you do it?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: We decided to do it at breakfast. Before anything else — before phones, before the kids' chaos starts. I look at her and say one thing I appreciate about her. She does the same for me. Takes maybe sixty seconds.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Monday was the hardest. We'd had a tense Sunday night — nothing major, just both tired and snippy. So Monday morning I'm sitting there thinking "what do I even appreciate right now?" and I almost said something sarcastic. But I made myself find something genuine.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What did you say?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I said "I appreciate that you got up with Mia last night when she had a nightmare. You didn't ask me to do it. You just got up." And I watched his whole body soften. Like physically his shoulders dropped.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It did. Because I had gotten up with Mia and I didn't think she'd noticed. I thought she was asleep through the whole thing. So hearing that she saw it and valued it — that felt really good. Like being seen.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's the core of the appreciation exercise — creating moments of feeling seen and valued. In Gottman's framework, we call these "turning toward" moments. Every time one partner makes a bid for connection and the other responds positively, you're depositing into the emotional bank account. Maria's acknowledgment of the nightmare moment was a deposit. Kevin, what did you say to her that morning?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I said "I appreciate how patient you are with Lucas's homework. I know he frustrates you but you never yell. You keep your cool." And she kind of teared up.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Because I DON'T feel patient. Inside I'm screaming. But he noticed the effort, not just the feeling. That mattered.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Beautiful. So even on a tense morning after a snippy night, you both found genuine positives. That's the practice. Over time, this trains your brain to scan for what's working rather than what's broken. Depression narrows focus to negatives. Relationship distress does the same thing — you become hypervigilant for faults. The appreciation ritual is a deliberate redirect. Now — let's check in on the four horsemen homework. Last session we identified that criticism and defensiveness were your primary patterns. Did you notice them this week?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I caught myself being critical on Monday night. Maria left the laundry in the dryer again — it had been sitting there since morning. And my first instinct was to say "you never follow through on anything." Which is criticism — the global "you never" character attack.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What did you do instead?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I literally bit the inside of my cheek for a second. Felt the impulse, let it pass. Then I said "I noticed the laundry is still in the dryer. Could you fold it when you get a chance?" Just the specific thing. No character judgment.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Excellent. That's what we call a complaint versus a criticism. A complaint addresses a specific behavior in a specific situation. A criticism attacks the person's character. "You left the laundry" versus "you never follow through." Maria, how did Kevin's rephrasing land for you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Hugely different. Night and day. When he says "you never follow through on anything," my defenses shoot up immediately. I start compiling a mental list of everything I DO follow through on — the kids' schedules, the bills, the groceries, his dry cleaning. I'm already building my defense case before he's finished the sentence. But when he just asked about the specific laundry? I could just say "oh right, I totally forgot — I'll grab it." No fight. Done in ten seconds. My blood pressure didn't even rise.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: This is the core Gottman principle at work. Criticism attacks character and triggers defensiveness. It pulls the other person into a courtroom — defense attorney mode. A complaint addresses a specific behavior and opens the door to simple problem-solving. Kevin, you rewired a habitual response in real time. How effortful was that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Very. It felt unnatural. Like biting my tongue literally. My whole body was ready to say the critical thing — it was right there on my lips. But the outcome was so dramatically better that I'm starting to internalize it. Like, the payoff is fast enough that my brain is going "oh, THIS way gets results."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's conditioning. Your brain is learning that specific language gets cooperation while global language gets warfare. Over time, the specific language will become more automatic. But for now, expect it to require effort. You're retraining years of habit. Maria, were there moments this week where you noticed the horsemen in YOUR communication?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. Wednesday night. Kevin came home late. He said he'd be home by seven — that's our agreement on weeknights because I'm alone with the kids from pickup to bedtime and by seven I'm running on fumes. He walked in at eight-thirty. No text. No call. Nothing. I was furious.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Tell me about the fury. What was it made of?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Anger on the surface. But underneath? Hurt. Feeling unimportant. Feeling like his work matters more than me. Feeling taken for granted. Like I'm the default parent and he doesn't even have to check in. My first instinct — and I literally had the words forming in my mouth — was contempt. I wanted to say something like "Nice of you to grace us with your presence. Clearly your family ranks somewhere below your email inbox."

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [winces] Yeah. I would have shut down completely if she'd said that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I believe you. Contempt is the most corrosive of the four horsemen. Gottman's research shows it's the single strongest predictor of divorce — more than criticism, defensiveness, or stonewalling. It communicates disgust. Superiority. "I'm above you." It's relationship acid. Maria, you felt that impulse. What did you do instead?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I took a breath. Literally held it for a count of four like you taught us. And I tried the soft startup. I said: "When you come home an hour and a half later than you said without texting, I feel unimportant and worried. I need you to communicate if plans change."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let me highlight what you did there. You named the specific behavior — "come home late without texting." You named your emotion — "unimportant and worried." And you stated a clear need — "communicate if plans change." No contempt. No character attack. No sarcasm. Kevin, how did that land?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I apologized. Immediately. I said "You're right. I got caught in a last-minute meeting that ran over and I should have texted. I'm sorry." And I meant it. She WAS right. When she comes at me with the contempt stuff — the sarcastic voice, the eye-roll — I go cold. I shut down. I stonewall. I literally can't speak. It's like my throat closes. But when she told me how she felt? I could hear her. I could respond. The wall didn't go up.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What you're describing, Kevin, is the physiological response we call "flooding." When contempt hits, your nervous system detects threat. Heart rate goes up, stress hormones flood your system, and the prefrontal cortex — your reasoning brain — goes offline. You stonewall because you literally cannot access language in that state. It's not willful. It's neurobiological. When Maria uses a soft startup instead, the threat detection doesn't activate and you remain in your social engagement system — able to hear, respond, repair.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So I'm not being a jerk when I stonewall? My mom always said my dad did that and she called it emotional abuse.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Stonewalling isn't malicious in most cases — it's a flooding response. It's your nervous system saying "too much, shutting down." That said, the IMPACT on your partner is still painful — to Maria, it feels like abandonment. Like she's talking to a wall. So we address both: Maria works on delivering messages without contempt, which prevents your flooding. And we give you tools for when flooding DOES happen — taking a break, self-soothing, returning to the conversation later. Both sides of the equation.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It's like we've been having the same fight for eight years — about the dishes, about his lateness, about the kids' schedules — and the content changes but the PATTERN is identical. I criticize or use contempt, he shuts down, I get more furious because he's silent, I escalate, he leaves the room, nothing gets resolved, resentment builds.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You just described what Gottman calls the "Four Horsemen Cascade" — criticism → defensiveness → contempt → stonewalling → nothing resolved → resentment → next fight starts from a higher baseline of anger. It's a degenerative spiral. Each unresolved fight makes the next one worse. What we're doing — the soft startups, the specific complaints, the appreciation deposits — is interrupting that cascade at multiple points. And you both did it this week. Multiple times.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Can I bring up something harder?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Of course. This is the space for hard things.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The sex thing. I don't know how else to say it. We haven't been intimate in... Maria, how long?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Six weeks. Maybe seven. Since before Easter.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: And it's not that I don't want to. I think about it all the time. But by the time the kids are down and we're finally alone at like 9:30, Maria's either exhausted or I'm anxious about getting rejected AGAIN, so neither of us initiates. We just watch our phones in bed and fall asleep on opposite sides.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Thank you for raising this, Kevin. I know it's vulnerable. Maria, what's your face saying right now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [sighs] I'm uncomfortable. But also relieved he brought it up because I think about it too. It's like this elephant in our bedroom. We both know it's there but neither of us wants to be the first to say it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Sexual intimacy is often a barometer of emotional intimacy. When couples are caught in negative cycles — criticism, defensiveness, disconnection — physical intimacy almost always declines. It's not a coincidence that the sex dropped off as the conflict increased. Maria, what's your perspective on what's happened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I DO want intimacy. I miss it. I miss feeling close to him. But honestly? When I've been feeling criticized and unappreciated all day — when I'm running the household alone and he comes home and the first thing he notices is what I DIDN'T do — the last thing I want is to be physically vulnerable with someone who made me feel small. Sex requires openness. And I can't be open when I feel defensive.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So for you, emotional safety is a prerequisite for physical vulnerability.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. Exactly. If the emotional connection is there — if I feel seen, appreciated, respected during the day — the physical will follow naturally. I WANT him. But he has to want ME — not just my body. The whole me. And sometimes it feels like the only time he's affectionate is when he wants sex. Like the tenderness is just a prelude to a transaction.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [hurt] That's not how I mean it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Hold on, Kevin. Let Maria finish and then you'll get your turn. Maria, say more about that feeling.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: If he comes up behind me in the kitchen and puts his arms around me, my first thought isn't "that's nice." It's "here we go — he wants sex tonight." And then I tense up. And then I pull away. And I see the hurt on his face and I feel guilty. But I can't help the reaction.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Kevin, your turn. What's your experience?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: For me, physical intimacy IS connection. That's how I feel loved. When Maria and I have sex, I feel close to her for days afterward. I'm a better husband, a more patient dad. When we're not having sex, I feel rejected and distant, and then I'm shorter with her, and then she's even less interested. It's a spiral. And what she just said — that she tenses up when I touch her — do you know how that feels? Like my wife is repulsed by me.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'm not repulsed by you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It FEELS that way.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Both of you — pause. This is the exact moment I want to work with. You're both naming something genuine and important. Maria needs emotional safety to be physically vulnerable. Kevin needs physical connection to feel emotionally secure. These aren't contradictory needs — they're complementary. But you're stuck in a chicken-and-egg deadlock: Kevin can't give emotional warmth without physical reassurance. Maria can't give physical openness without emotional warmth. Someone has to go first. Actually — both of you need to go first, simultaneously, in your own language.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: How do we do that?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's start with rebuilding what I call the "bridge" between your two love languages. Kevin, your primary language is physical touch. Maria, yours sounds like words of affirmation and acts of service. Right now, Kevin is primarily speaking his own language at Maria — reaching for touch, hoping for sex. Maria is primarily feeling unloved in her language — she wants verbal appreciation and shared domestic load. Neither of you is getting what you need because you're speaking different languages. The daily appreciations are already addressing Maria's language. Kevin, when you consistently show Maria that you see her, value her, acknowledge her — outside the bedroom, with no strings attached — her safety increases.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. That's exactly it. When he says something appreciative at breakfast, I feel warmer toward him all day. It's not even about the words — it's about being seen.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And Maria — I want to challenge you gently here. Kevin expressed that physical closeness is how HE feels loved. When you pull away from his touch in the kitchen, even though your reason is valid, the impact on him is "my wife doesn't want me." Can you make space for smaller physical gestures that don't carry the weight of "this must lead to sex"? A hand on his back. Holding hands on the couch. A real kiss hello — not a peck, but a four-second kiss. These feed his attachment system without requiring you to be sexually vulnerable before you're emotionally ready.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I could do that. I think the problem is that ANY touch has become loaded. Even holding hands feels like it carries this unspoken question: "so are we going to tonight?" And that pressure makes me avoid all of it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I probably send that signal without meaning to. Like — if she touches me, I immediately think "oh, maybe tonight" and my energy shifts and she picks up on it and retreats.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's incredibly insightful, Kevin. You're right — and Maria confirmed it. Every physical interaction has become weighed down with sexual expectation and the potential for rejection. The solution is counterintuitive: we need to explicitly take sex OFF the table temporarily.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: What do you mean, off the table?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I want to recommend what I call "sensate focus" or "intimacy without intercourse" as a homework exercise. Two or three times this week, I want you to spend twenty to thirty minutes in physical closeness — massage, cuddling on the couch, spooning in bed, back rubs, whatever feels good — with intercourse explicitly, mutually, agreed-upon OFF THE TABLE. It's not going to lead to sex. Period. This removes the performance pressure for both of you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [exhaling] Oh. I could do that. Actually that sounds really nice. It's the pressure of "this has to lead somewhere" that makes me avoid any physical contact. If I KNOW it's just touch for the sake of touch...

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Right. And Kevin — this serves you too, even though it might feel frustrating initially. Because right now you're getting ZERO physical contact. If we remove the sex pressure, Maria can relax into touch, which means you get SOME physical intimacy. And over time, as she feels safe that touch won't always be a demand, she'll be more likely to initiate something beyond cuddling. The paradox is: the less you pressure, the more she approaches.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That... makes sense logically. My worry is that we'll just get stuck in cuddling-only mode forever.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It won't be forever. This is a bridge — a temporary intervention to rebuild trust in physical contact. Most couples who do this find that sexual desire returns naturally within two to four weeks because the anxiety has been removed from the equation. Maria, does that feel accurate?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. If I could touch him without wondering "is this going to turn into something I have to do?" — if touch could just be connection, warmth, safety — I think my desire would come back on its own. It's not gone. It's just behind a wall of pressure.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. I can do this. Twenty minutes of cuddling, no sex. And I'll keep my energy in check — not giving off the "but what about..." vibe.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. And talk about it openly. Before you start, say to each other: "this is just connection. Nothing more. We're safe." Name it explicitly. Now — I want to go back to the communication piece for a moment because it connects. Maria, let's practice a soft startup for the intimacy issue. Imagine Kevin hasn't touched you in three days and you're feeling distant. How would you bring it up using the framework?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Um... "When we haven't had physical closeness in a few days, I start to feel disconnected from you. I miss it. Could we make time to just be together tonight?"

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Beautiful. Kevin, how does that land?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like a gift. She's saying she misses ME. She's asking for closeness. I would drop everything for that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Now Kevin, your turn. Imagine you're feeling sexually frustrated but you know sex is off the table. How do you express that need without pressure?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [thinking] "I'm feeling disconnected physically and I want to feel close to you. I'm not asking for anything specific — I just miss being in your space. Can we spend some time together tonight?"

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Maria?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's... yeah. I'd respond to that. No defenses. Just warmth. I'd say "yes, come here."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: See the difference? When the ask is for CONNECTION rather than for PERFORMANCE, the other person can say yes freely. Nobody wants to feel demanded of. Everyone wants to feel wanted.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I've been demanding without realizing it. Like — not verbally demanding. But energetically.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Right. The unspoken demand creates pressure, which creates avoidance, which creates more frustration, which creates more demand-energy. It's another cycle. Breaking it requires naming needs softly, accepting "not tonight" without withdrawing emotionally, and trusting that the connection will build over time. Kevin, here's a homework for you specifically: when Maria says "not tonight" to anything physical — or even just doesn't initiate — your job is to NOT withdraw. Stay warm. Stay present. Don't punish her with distance. Can you do that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's hard. Because when she pulls away, my instinct is to protect myself by pulling away too.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I know. That's the avoidant-anxious dance you two do. You're the anxious one in this dynamic — reaching, needing reassurance. She's more avoidant — needing space, feeling smothered by demand. When you pull away after rejection, she doesn't feel relief — she feels guilt. And guilt doesn't generate desire. Warmth generates desire. Staying present after a "no" is one of the most powerful things you can do for your relationship.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet] Okay. I'll try. Stay warm even when it hurts.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [reaching for his hand] I do want you. I need you to know that. This isn't about not wanting you. It's about feeling safe enough to let go.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [squeezing her hand] I hear you. I'll work on making you feel safe.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: This is a beautiful moment. What you're doing right now — the hand-holding, the eye contact, the verbal reassurance — this IS the work. It's not just something that happens in session. Let this be your mode at home too.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It's like we're finally learning HOW to be married instead of just being married badly for eight years.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Eight years of pattern doesn't rewrite in seven sessions. But what you're doing is learning the mechanics of repair and connection. Every couple fights — even the happiest couples have perpetual problems that never fully resolve. The difference between masters and disasters of relationship — Gottman's terms — isn't the absence of conflict. It's the ability to repair afterward, to turn toward each other instead of away, and to maintain a ratio of five positive interactions to every one negative.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Five to one. You mentioned that before. I've been trying to count. I think we're at maybe... three to one right now?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Three to one is already significant progress. When you started therapy, you were probably at one to one or worse — every interaction was fraught. Getting to five-to-one takes time. The appreciation ritual is one daily deposit. But look for opportunities throughout the day: a text that says "thinking of you," a compliment about dinner, saying "thank you" when she hands you coffee, physical affection with no agenda.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I need to do that too. I get so focused on what Kevin does wrong that I forget to notice what he does right. Like — he coaches Lucas's soccer on Saturdays. He takes the kids to school every morning so I can sleep an extra thirty minutes. He always fills my gas tank. Those are love acts. I should say so.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Write them down if that helps. Some couples keep a gratitude list about their partner — at the end of each day, write one thing they did well. It rewires the negativity bias that builds up during conflict.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'd like that. Even just hearing her say those things just now — the soccer, the gas tank — I lit up inside. Like, you SEE me. You notice.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's the human need for recognition. Both of you need it. Both of you can give it. It costs nothing and deposits are enormous.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Why didn't we learn this earlier? Like, why isn't this taught somewhere?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a great question. Most of us inherited our communication patterns from watching our parents — who also weren't taught. Kevin, you mentioned your dad stonewalled. That's where you learned it. Maria, your model?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My mom was the critical one. She criticized my dad constantly — nothing was ever good enough. Sound familiar? [rueful laugh] I became my mother.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And that awareness — "I'm repeating my mother's pattern" — is the first step to choosing differently. You can't change what you can't see. Now you see it. And you're choosing differently.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My dad would just go silent. For days sometimes. And my mom would cry and beg him to talk. I swore I'd never be like him. And then I do exactly the same thing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Breaking intergenerational patterns is some of the hardest work in therapy. But you're both doing it — Kevin by staying present instead of stonewalling, Maria by softening her approach instead of criticizing. You're giving your children a different model than what you received. That's legacy work.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [emotional] I don't want Mia to grow up thinking that's how couples talk to each other.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: She's five. She won't remember the worst of it. She'll remember the repair. She'll remember watching mommy and daddy choose kindness over cruelty. That's what you're building now.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. I'm emotional. Let's get to homework before I completely lose it. [laughs]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [smiles] Alright. Let me summarize this week's assignments. One: continue daily appreciation check-ins at breakfast. Two: practice soft startups when issues arise — specific behavior, your feeling, your need. Three: non-sexual physical intimacy exercise — cuddling, massage, closeness — at least twice this week, with sex explicitly off the table. Before you start, say out loud to each other: "this is just connection." Four: Kevin, practice staying warm and present after any form of "no" from Maria — no withdrawing. Five: Maria, initiate at least two non-sexual physical touches per day — hand on back, real kiss, sitting close. Feed Kevin's attachment system with small gestures.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Got it all. I'm actually looking forward to trying the cuddling thing. No pressure sounds... peaceful actually.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Me too. I think once the pressure is gone, I'll actually enjoy being close again. I miss it. I miss us.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're finding your way back to "us." It takes time and it takes practice. But the fact that you're both here, both willing, both reaching toward each other — that's what matters. Not perfection. Progress. I'll see you next Thursday.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr. Davis. This session felt important. Like a turning point.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Same. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You did the work. I just held the space. See you next week.

goals: Communication breakdown — Gottman method four horsemen
